askNext: What information qualifies for memory, and what must never be stored?

memory-policy.body: Memory policy matters because forgetting is part of quality control. A system that never forgets is often just a system that never learned restraint.
memory-policy.example: "The assistant felt personal right up until its memory policy allowed one outdated preference to haunt every recommendation for the next three months."
memory-policy.misuse: The term gets reduced to "we have memory now", as if any retained context were inherently intelligent. It also gets used to dress up ordinary logging or vector storage without answering the harder questions about consent, correction, deletion, and sensitive data.
memory-policy.practicalMeaning: In practice it means retention windows, summarisation rules, provenance, retrieval thresholds, redaction filters, user controls, and clear logic for when stale memory should be ignored rather than honoured out of misplaced loyalty.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: The rules that make continuity feel helpful instead of mildly creepy.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A set of retention, retrieval, and deletion decisions nobody can safely leave to improvisation.

memory-policy.warningLabel: Useful until it starts remembering the wrong thing with perfect confidence.
memory-policy.whyExists: It exists because continuity is useful and indiscriminate memory is dangerous. Long-running systems can become more helpful by remembering preferences, facts, and prior decisions, but they can also become more wrong, more invasive, and more difficult to debug.

mistral-ai.body: It is one of the clearest examples of how "open" in AI rarely means simple purity. It usually means a strategy, a pricing posture, and a market wedge wearing noble clothes.

mistral-ai.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a vendor choice for teams that want competitive models, deployment flexibility, open-weight options, and some combination of cost discipline, control, and distance from the biggest US platforms.

mistral-ai.whyExists: Because the market wanted proof that serious model-building could happen outside the dominant US labs, and because Mistral made strategic use of openness, efficiency, and European identity to become that proof.

mixture-of-experts.body: The name flatters the machinery. What matters is not that there are experts, but whether the router sends work to the right places often enough to justify the complications.
mixture-of-experts.example: "The model was a mixture of experts, which sounded philosophical until the serving team had to explain the routing bottlenecks."
mixture-of-experts.misuse: By treating "experts" as if the model contains little professors with stable domains of knowledge. It is an optimisation strategy, not a panel discussion. The routing can help a great deal, but it does not grant wisdom by committee.
mixture-of-experts.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means better parameter-efficiency on paper, more complex training and serving behaviour, routing overhead, and a fresh set of engineering questions about load balancing, latency spikes, and uneven quality.

askNext: How much of the training mix is synthetic, and how is its quality measured?
What prevents generated noise from becoming self-confirming doctrine?
Are we preserving human-created edge cases or optimising them out of existence?
model-collapse.body: The phrase has become a general anxiety container, which is understandable. Still, the useful question is not whether collapse is coming in the abstract, but whether your data pipeline has the manners to notice contamination before it institutionalises it.
model-collapse.example: "The lab was not worried about synthetic data in principle. It was worried about model collapse from feeding the next system a buffet of the previous system's bad habits."
model-collapse.misuse: By treating it as a guaranteed apocalypse or as proof that synthetic data is worthless. Synthetic data can be valuable. The concern is about unmanaged feedback loops, weak provenance, and replacing judgement with volume.
model-collapse.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means teams need stronger data lineage, better curation, and honest evaluation before they let generated material dominate the training mix just because it is abundant and conveniently machine-readable.

translations.label: internet meaning
translations.text: The machines are eating too much of their own content and may start to resemble it.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Poorly managed recursive data pipelines can flatten quality faster than the dashboards admit.

model-collapse.whyExists: Because synthetic data is useful, cheap, and increasingly tempting at scale, but feedback loops can distort distributions, wash out edge cases, and quietly teach systems to imitate their own errors.

askNext: Which part of the system changed: the base model, the prompt stack, the tools, the routing, or the traffic?
What stable evaluation set shows the behaviour actually moved rather than merely encountering a harder week?

model-drift.body: Model drift deserves a score of five because teams confuse the label with the diagnosis. The phrase is useful only if it narrows the search rather than sanctifying the shrug.
model-drift.example: "The team called it model drift because that sounded dignified. The incident report later revealed a prompt change, a tool timeout, and optimism."
model-drift.misuse: It gets used as a polite synonym for "something got worse". Sometimes the issue is genuine behaviour drift. Sometimes it is bad retrieval, a broken tool, a prompt edit, or a team discovering that last month's benchmark did not survive contact with this month's traffic.
model-drift.practicalMeaning: In practice it means running comparisons over time, holding out stable evaluation sets, watching sensitive slices rather than averages, and keeping enough version history to identify whether the model changed or the system around it did.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The system changed somewhere, and now you must prove where before someone blames the wrong component.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A behaviour change whose cause may live in the model, the scaffolding, or the surrounding workflow.

model-drift.whyExists: AI systems are not static artefacts. Providers update models, teams revise prompts, tools change their schemas, and user behaviour wanders off. The term exists because quality can move even when nobody thinks they touched the main lever.

model-garden.misuse: By making model access sound like curation solved the hard part. A catalogue is useful. It does not answer which model is trustworthy, economical, regionally available, or appropriate for a real workload.
model-garden.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a procurement-and-deployment layer with billing, quotas, IAM, regions, logging, and just enough abstraction to turn model shopping into infrastructure policy.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A cloud-controlled shelf of models that turns experimentation into something procurement can invoice.

model-garden.whyExists: Because buyers want more than one model, clouds want to be the place where that choice gets made, and nobody enjoys integrating a fresh API every time the leaderboard twitches.

model-router.body: The appeal is obvious. So is the trap. Once routing becomes strategic, the application has acquired a small foreign policy department.

model-router.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means heuristics, policy rules, retries, provider abstractions, observability, and a permanent negotiation between quality, speed, and the monthly bill.

askNext: Which behaviours in this product come from our prompt stack, and which come from the provider's published rules?
How closely does the live model match the current spec in the cases we actually care about?

model-spec.body: Model specs matter because the era of purely generic models is over. The model arrives with prior instructions, prior politics, and prior notions of what counts as responsible behaviour.
model-spec.example: "The product team called it a bug. The model spec called it a refusal. The user called it a waste of time."
model-spec.misuse: It gets treated as a guarantee, a safety proof, or a substitute for evaluation. A spec can clarify intent. It cannot ensure that every model version, product surface, or edge case will obey the text as faithfully as legal and marketing might prefer.
model-spec.practicalMeaning: In practice it means reading the provider's stated priorities, understanding how instruction hierarchy and refusals are supposed to work, and designing prompts, product expectations, and escalation paths around the fact that the model has house rules you did not personally write.

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: The provider's declared behavioural contract, minus the comforting fantasy that declarations enforce themselves.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A document you should read before accusing the model of breaking rules that were never yours to set.

model-spec.whyExists: Modern models are expected to be useful, safe, steerable, and publicly defensible at once. A specification exists so providers can state what behaviour they are trying to produce and give developers a reference for the rules sitting behind the pleasant interface.

askNext: Which modalities can the system actually handle in production?
Does the model merely accept multiple formats, or does it use them jointly to improve the outcome?
How has performance been evaluated across the different input types?
multimodal.body: The adjective matters less than the task. The real question is whether the extra modality materially improves the outcome or simply decorates the feature list.
multimodal.example: "The feature was multimodal in the useful sense: it could read the chart, listen to the spoken explanation, and answer questions about the mismatch."
multimodal.misuse: It gets waved around as a prestige adjective even when a product merely accepts an image upload and produces a caption. True multimodal capability is not just accepting multiple inputs. It is being able to use them meaningfully for the task at hand.
multimodal.note: Multimodal can describe both input and output capabilities. A system that reads images and writes text is multimodal, even if it does not generate images in return.
multimodal.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a model or stack that can ingest several types of data, map them into compatible internal representations, and produce outputs that reflect those combined signals. The hard part is often not modality support itself but maintaining reliability across them.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The system works across more than one type of data, with all the messy evaluation problems that implies.

multimodal.whyExists: Human tasks rarely arrive as clean text alone. Multimodal systems exist because many useful applications involve documents, screenshots, spoken language, visual context, or combinations of these.

mustafa-suleyman.body: It is easier to mock the companion idea than to notice how many major products are inching toward it anyway.
mustafa-suleyman.example: "The roadmap had gone full Mustafa: less tool, more companion, with enough memory and polish to make dependence sound like convenience."
mustafa-suleyman.misuse: By treating "companion" language as either obviously profound or obviously absurd. It is better understood as a serious product thesis about persistence, personalisation, convenience, and software that wants to sit between the user and everything else.
mustafa-suleyman.practicalMeaning: When people invoke Mustafa Suleyman, they usually mean AI as a persistent product layer: more assistant, less query box; more relationship, less transaction.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A shorthand for the thesis that AI should feel less like software you visit and more like software that stays with you.

mustafa-suleyman.whyExists: Because AI is not only a research contest. It is also a packaging contest, an interface contest, and a trust contest. Suleyman's name often marks the point where the conversation shifts from raw capability to how intelligence is turned into habit.
